versionNotes: Sprint 4 co-design — students help shape Sprint 4 through 4 aspects with individual reflection + partner discussion
title: Sprint 4 Co-Design
description: Help design Sprint 4. For each of 4 aspects, reflect individually, then discuss with a partner. Switch partners after Aspect 2.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 1: What Should We Be Able to Do?

Think beyond specific assignments. After 16 weeks in this course, what should every student be able to do or demonstrate that they could not before? Think about capabilities that would matter to an employer, to your career, to your ability to solve real problems.

Instructions: Write your individual response first. You will share and discuss with a partner next.
questions.prompt: What should every student who completes this course be able to do or demonstrate? Name 2-3 specific capabilities — not topics covered, but things you can actually DO.
questions.placeholder: I think every student should be able to walk into a domain they know nothing about — like we did with MDRO — and within a week have a specific problem staked out, a user identified, and a first build started. Not because the build is good, but because they know how to start without waiting for permission. The second capability is being able to show their thinking to someone else — not just the final product, but the decisions they made and why. Piranavan could follow my reasoning even when my solution was wrong, and that mattered more than getting it right on the first try.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 1

Turn to the person next to you. Share your response. Listen to theirs. Then discuss together:

  • Where do your answers overlap? Where do they differ?
  • Think about the entire class — would your answers serve everyone, or just people like you?
  • What capability would you add after hearing your partner?
questions.prompt: After discussing with your partner, write down: (1) your partner's name, (2) the capabilities you both agreed matter most, and (3) anything that changed in your thinking.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Daniel Orta. We both agreed that the most important capability is being able to frame a problem for someone who isn't technical — like writing a stake that Piranavan could actually respond to. Daniel added something I hadn't thought about: the ability to know when to stop researching and start building. He said he spent too long in the Understand phase and it hurt his v1. That changed my thinking — I was going to say 'deep research skills' but now I think it's more about knowing when you have enough to act.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 2: What Activities Help Us Get There?

Given what you said we should learn in Aspect 1 — what types of activities would actually develop those abilities? Think about what has worked so far in this course and what has not. Consider both in-class sessions and homework.

Instructions: Write your individual response. Same partner discussion follows.
questions.prompt: What types of activities — in class and as homework — would help develop the capabilities you identified? Name at least one activity type that has worked well this semester and one that has not.
questions.placeholder: The Dojo worked well for me — having AI challenge my problem stake before I brought it to Piranavan meant I had already thought through the obvious objections. The peer interviews in Week 10 also worked because my partner asked a question I hadn't considered ('what happens when there's no nurse available?'). What didn't work as well: some of the written reflections felt like I was just restating what I already knew. I'd rather have a short reflection tied to a specific decision I made that week, like 'name one thing you changed after feedback and why.'

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 2

Share your response. Then discuss:

  • Which activities from Sprints 1-3 actually changed how you think or work?
  • Which felt like busywork?
  • If you could design one in-class activity and one homework activity for Sprint 4, what would they be?
questions.prompt: After discussing: (1) your partner's name (same partner), (2) activities you agreed work best, (3) what you would change or add for Sprint 4.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Daniel Orta. We agreed the expert sessions with Piranavan were the most valuable — having to present your thinking to someone who actually knows the domain forces you to be specific. For Sprint 4, we'd want more of that: structured time with a real stakeholder, not just peers. One thing we'd change: instead of separate build plan and build v1 submissions, combine them — the plan changes so fast once you start building that the plan submission felt outdated by the time v1 was due.

questions.infoBlock:
Switch partners. Find a different person to sit with for Aspects 3 and 4.

Aspect 3: How Do We Know We Got There?

Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense. The defense is where you prove your growth across all 4 sprints.

Failing and learning from it IS the learning. A student who built something that broke, understood why, and changed direction has stronger evidence of growth than one whose first attempt happened to work. The defense should reward honest struggle — not just polished outcomes. How should we assess whether you actually developed the capabilities from Aspect 1? What counts as evidence? What format lets you show your growth most honestly?
questions.prompt: How should we assess your learning — what counts as real evidence of growth? And what defense format would let you show that growth most effectively? Consider: presentations, portfolio walkthroughs, peer challenges, Q&A sessions, or something else.
questions.placeholder: The strongest evidence would be showing the delta — where my thinking was in Week 1 versus where it is now. Not a polished final product, but the messy middle: the problem stake that Piranavan tore apart, the v1 that failed the self-test, and what I did differently in v2. A portfolio walkthrough where I narrate the arc would let me show that. The format should let the student talk through decisions, not just present slides — maybe a 10-minute walkthrough with live Q&A from peers who can actually challenge the reasoning.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 3

Share with your NEW partner. Discuss:

  • What kind of evidence would convince a skeptic that you actually grew? Remember: showing what failed and what you learned from it is not a weakness — it IS the evidence.
  • Should the defense be 1-on-1 with the instructor, in small groups presenting each other's work, or something else?
  • Think about what would work for the whole class, not just you.
questions.prompt: After discussing with your NEW partner: (1) new partner's name, (2) what evidence format you agreed on, (3) what defense structure would work best for the class.
questions.placeholder: New partner: Victoria Cimino. We agreed that the best evidence is showing specific moments where your thinking changed — not just 'I learned a lot' but 'here's the exact feedback from Piranavan that made me redesign the triage flow.' Victoria suggested small groups of 3-4 where each person walks through their arc and the others ask questions. That feels better than 1-on-1 with the instructor because peers catch different things — they know what the assignments actually felt like. We'd want the defense to explicitly ask: 'show us something that didn't work and what you did about it.'

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 4: What Should Sprint 4 Look Like?

Sprint 4 is weeks 13-16. You have the option to stay with your MDRO stakeholder or choose a new one (with instructor approval). Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense. Given everything you discussed in Aspects 1-3, what should Sprint 4 include to best prepare you?
questions.prompt: What should Sprint 4 look like? Consider: Do you want to stay with MDRO or switch stakeholders? What weekly structure would help? What would make the final 4 weeks feel like they matter?
questions.placeholder: I'd want to stay with MDRO — I finally understand the domain well enough to build something real, and switching now would mean starting over with the Understand phase. Weekly structure: Week 13 should be goal-setting and stakeholder confirmation. Weeks 14-15 should be build time with at least one more expert session. Week 16 is defense. What would make it matter: having Piranavan actually use or react to what we built. If the final product just gets graded and filed away, it feels like an assignment. If someone outside the class sees it, it feels like real work.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 4

Share with same partner as Aspects 3-4. Discuss:

  • If you designed Sprint 4 for the whole class, what would the non-negotiables be?
  • What should be flexible?
  • One concrete recommendation to Prof. Sathya.
questions.prompt: Final discussion capture: (1) partner's name, (2) your group's top recommendation for Sprint 4, (3) one thing that should definitely stay and one thing that should definitely change.
questions.placeholder: Partner: Victoria Cimino. Top recommendation: make the portfolio defense include at least one artifact that a real stakeholder has seen and responded to — not just peer-reviewed work. Keep: the expert access sessions and the Dojo format — both pushed us harder than any written assignment. Change: reduce the number of separate submissions. Instead of build plan + build v1 + build review as three separate things, make it one living document that gets updated. The submission overhead pulled time away from actual building.
